name: Blawn Mountain
short_description: BLM rockhounding site near Beaver offering agate, chalcedony, and jasper collecting. Public land with no permit required and a 25 lb daily limit.
description:
Blawn Mountain sits on Bureau of Land Management property in Beaver County, Utah, offering accessible rockhounding for collectors seeking agates and chalcedony specimens. The site is known for producing quality quartz-based gems found in the volcanic and mineralized soils of the region. Collectors typically work washes and exposed hillsides where weathering has exposed attractive specimens. The surrounding terrain provides excellent views of the High Plateaus geology that shaped this collecting area. Spring and fall offer the most comfortable collecting conditions, though the location remains accessible much of the year for dedicated rockhounders.

The geology of Blawn Mountain reflects Utah's volcanic heritage. During the Tertiary Period, extensive volcanic activity across the region deposited silica-rich materials and created the mineralized zones where agates and chalcedony nodules formed. These gems developed within vesicles (gas pockets) in ancient basaltic and rhyolitic flows, then were exposed through millions of years of erosion. The area's high elevation and seasonal precipitation continue to weather specimens free from the surrounding host rock, making surface collecting viable.

Access to Blawn Mountain is straightforward for most rockhounders. The location is not heavily traveled, meaning you may find material that larger sites have exhausted. Focus your effort on washes where runoff concentrates loose specimens, and examine the bases of exposed outcrops where material naturally collects. The best finds often come from systematic searching rather than random exploration.

This site works well for families and intermediate collectors. The open BLM land allows flexible access and exploration without the constraints of fee dig sites. Bring plenty of water, sunscreen, and wear sturdy boots as the terrain can be rocky and uneven. Early morning or late afternoon collecting provides relief from direct sun exposure.
history: Blawn Mountain lies within Utah's High Plateaus province, where Tertiary-age volcanism (30-40 million years ago) deposited thick sequences of basalt and rhyolite flows rich in silica and other minerals. As these volcanic rocks weathered and fractured, circulating groundwater deposited silica-rich solutions into cavities, forming the agate and chalcedony nodules found at the surface today. The area has long been known to local rockhounders as a reliable source for quality quartz-based specimens.
tips: Search the dry washes and arroyos where seasonal water flow concentrates loose agates and chalcedony pieces. Look for specimens with translucent or banded coloration ranging from white to orange, gray, or brown tones - these indicate quality material worth collecting. Bring a classifier or small mesh screen to sift loose material in productive areas. The exposed hillsides above washes often show the parent material still in place, so check the weathered surfaces and fracture lines where specimens are naturally being exposed.
terrain_notes: Elevation approximately 9,000 feet. Terrain consists of open sagebrush slopes with scattered rock outcrops and seasonal washes. Slopes range from gentle to moderately steep with loose volcanic rock and scattered boulders. Walking distance from parking area varies from immediate access to 1-2 miles for prime collecting zones. Footing is rocky with loose scree common; sturdy hiking boots are essential. Vegetation is sparse at higher elevations with scattered juniper and pinyon pine.
best_season: May - October. High elevation means winter snow blocks access November through April. Late spring through early fall provides the most comfortable conditions for extended collecting, with June through September offering the longest daylight hours.
written_directions: From Beaver, take US 191 south approximately 8 miles. Look for BLM access roads or parking areas marked for recreation on the west side of the highway in the Blawn Mountain area. Follow any visible dirt roads or established access routes toward the mountain. Coordinates GPS to 38.254551, -113.579374 for precise navigation. Four-wheel drive may be helpful but passenger vehicles can typically access main parking areas.
road_conditions: Dirt roads to access areas, may be rough or washboarded depending on season. Passenger vehicles can reach main areas; high clearance recommended.
parking_notes: Informal pullouts and parking areas scattered along access roads. No developed facilities. Space typically available even during busy seasons due to the site's relatively low popularity.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). No commercial collection. No motorized excavation or heavy equipment. All collecting must be done by hand tools only. Respect any posted closures or restrictions.
rules:
Personal use only
25 lb daily limit per person
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Respect all posted closures
nearest_services: Beaver, Utah - approximately 8 miles north. Full services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Moderate terrain with uneven rocky footing and loose scree. No wheelchair access to collecting areas. Sturdy hiking boots required. Slopes are negotiable for most hikers but not suitable for those with limited mobility. Elevation of 9,000 feet may affect those sensitive to altitude.

faq.question: What types of agates and gems can I find at Blawn Mountain in Utah?
faq.answer: Blawn Mountain is known for producing agate, chalcedony, jasper, and quartz specimens. Most finds feature translucent to semi-opaque material with banding in white, gray, orange, and brown tones. The volcanic geology of the area creates conditions favorable for silica-based gems in various color combinations.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Blawn Mountain?
faq.answer: No permit is required for recreational collecting at Blawn Mountain. The site is on Bureau of Land Management (BLM) public land where personal use collection is allowed under the Federal Land Policy and Management Act (FLPMA). Simply follow the 25 lb daily limit and collect by hand tools only.

faq.question: Is Blawn Mountain rockhounding free, or is there a collection fee?
faq.answer: Blawn Mountain is completely free to rockhound. There is no fee, no permit cost, and no daily entrance charge. It is open to the public as BLM land dedicated to recreational use. You only need to follow collection rules regarding weight limits and collection methods.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound at Blawn Mountain near Beaver?
faq.answer: The best season is May through October. Winter snow typically blocks access from November through April due to the site's 9,000-foot elevation. Late spring through early fall provides optimal collecting conditions with comfortable temperatures and reliable access to washes and hillsides.

faq.question: Can I bring my family and dog to rockhound at Blawn Mountain?
faq.answer: Yes, Blawn Mountain is family-friendly and dog-friendly. The open BLM terrain allows dogs to accompany you, and the moderate difficulty makes it suitable for children ages 8 and up. Bring plenty of water for both people and pets, as the site is somewhat remote with no developed facilities.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Blawn Mountain?
faq.answer: Bring a small hand tool set including a digging tool or pick, classifier or mesh screen, sturdy hiking boots, sunscreen, and at least 2 liters of water per person. A loupe or magnifying glass helps identify quality specimens. Hand tools only are allowed - no motorized equipment. A backpack and collecting bags will help you carry specimens up to the 25 lb daily limit.

meta_description: Find agate, chalcedony, and quartz at Blawn Mountain, Utah. Free BLM rockhounding near Beaver with no permit required. 25 lb daily limit.
